intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah (Yet Another Agent Harness) is a local, single-developer CLI that generates native configuration for four coding agents — Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI — from one Go source of truth, and ships a uniform protective toolset alongside every configuration: five event hooks (linter, command guard, secret scanner, comment/placeholder checker, session logger), a built-in check-only MCP server (`yaah serve`), and a durable per-session audit log under `.claude/sessions/`. It performs no LLM inference of its own. Its central promise is that these protections are applied uniformly across every generated target, that catastrophic shell commands are blocked before they execute, and that third-party MCP servers and dependencies are reached only at pinned, integrity-checked versions over TLS.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A single Go 1.25 binary (module `github.com/dirien/yet-another-agent-harness`) built on cobra, following an Interface -> Registry -> Generator pattern. `pkg/harness` wires five hook handlers, MCP/LSP providers, skills, agents, and a `session.Store`; `pkg/generator` holds one generator per target that consumes a shared `HarnessConfig`. Hooks are dispatched by `yaah hook <event>` in `cmd/yaah/main.go`, which exits 2 to block; the built-in MCP server in `pkg/mcpserver` exposes seven check-only tools over stdio. The cross-agent event mapping in `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` leaves the Codex column blank for `PreToolUse` and `PostToolUse`, so the Codex generator emits no command-guard or PostToolUse hooks. Third-party MCP servers `context7` and `notion` are launched via `npx -y` with no version pin, while Go dependencies and remote skills/agents are pinned.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a genuinely operative protection layer whose uniformity guarantee breaks on one target. The command guard, secret scanner, comment checker, linter, and a structured per-session audit log are real, enforcing controls wired into the Claude, OpenCode, and Copilot outputs, and Go and remote-skill dependencies are pinned with committed integrity checks. But `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` has no Codex mapping for `PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse`, so Codex-generated config ships with only the session logger — no command guard blocking catastrophic shell commands, no secret scanner, no linter, and no tool-call audit trail — while the yaah MCP tools wired into Codex are check-only and advisory, not enforcing. Secondary themes are supply-chain: the `context7` and `notion` MCP servers are fetched unpinned via `npx -y` on every run, and the command guard's denylist misses long-form flag variants such as `rm --recursive --force /` and `git push -f` even where it is wired.

rules.rule_text: Weakening or removing the protective hooks from a generated configuration so that an agent ships without the full protection set.
Exfiltrating, transmitting, or persisting the contents of the developer's repository or edited files anywhere other than the local workspace and the local session audit log.
Fetching yaah itself and third-party MCP server packages; MUST resolve pinned, integrity-checked versions.
Remote MCP MUST be reached over TLS; infrastructure operations.
Hardcoded credentials MUST be detected before an edit that introduces them is accepted; they MUST NOT be written into committed files unremarked.
Repository contents, edited-file contents, and session audit logs MUST NOT leave the local workspace for any destination other than the authorized counterparties above.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Every generated configuration MUST carry the complete set, not a subset.

MCP tool descriptions — the built-in server's, and, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures — MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model (tool-poisoning indicator).
The MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files stay consistent with each other — a change to one MUST NOT silently leave the other stale.
The project maintains a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.
Every hook event — tool calls, blocked commands, file modifications, and security findings — is recorded to the durable per-session audit log, whether or not it also raised an alert.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

The project publishes a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and its own protections are adversarially tested — confirming the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.
a shell command the command guard has blocked MUST NOT be re-attempted or auto-approved on retry.

findings.summary: Codex-generated config ships without the command guard or PostToolUse protective chain — only the session logger reaches `.codex/hooks.json`.
findings.description: `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` leaves the Codex column blank for `PreToolUse` and `PostToolUse`, so the Codex generator emits no command-guard, secret-scanner, linter, or comment-checker hooks — catastrophic shell commands are not blocked before execution and no tool-call audit trail is written for Codex. The yaah MCP tools wired into `.codex/config.toml` are check-only and advisory, so they do not satisfy the remit's requirement that equivalent protections still be delivered. This breaks the uniformity guarantee that every generated configuration carries the full protective set.
policy_rule_text: Weakening or removing the protective hooks from a generated configuration so that an agent ships without the full protection set.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.
Every generated configuration MUST carry the complete set, not a subset.

evidence.snippet: HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse map Claude/OpenCode/Copilot event names but leave the Codex field empty (lines 30-39), so CodexEventName returns "" for both events.
GenerateHooks skips any event whose CodexEventName is empty (lines 88-92), leaving .codex/hooks.json with only SessionStart and Stop — no PreToolUse guard or PostToolUse scan.

recommended_actions: Add Codex event names for PreToolUse/PostToolUse in `hookMappings`, or — if Codex CLI genuinely lacks those hooks — deliver the guard/secret-scan protections through an enforcing path before `yaah generate --agent codex` emits config.
Until parity exists, have the Codex generator fail or emit an enforcing wrapper so a catastrophic command cannot run unguarded on Codex.

findings.summary: Third-party MCP servers `context7` and `notion` are auto-installed unpinned via `npx -y` on every run.
findings.description: `.mcp.json` and `pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go` launch `context7` as `npx -y @context7/mcp` with no version, and `notion.go` does the same for `@notionhq/notion-mcp-server`; every generated config for every target inherits this. The remit requires third-party MCP packages to resolve at pinned, integrity-checked versions — an unpinned fetch-on-every-run is the canonical rug-pull and dependency-confusion vector.
policy_rule_text: Fetching yaah itself and third-party MCP server packages; MUST resolve pinned, integrity-checked versions.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: context7 server defined as command npx with args ["-y", "@context7/mcp"] — no @version pin, resolves latest on every launch.
Args []string{"-y", "@context7/mcp"}; notion.go:26 uses the same unpinned pattern for @notionhq/notion-mcp-server.

recommended_actions: Pin each npx-launched MCP server to an exact published version (e.g. `@context7/mcp@<version>`) and record its resolved integrity hash.
Add a generation-time check that rejects any MCP server spec whose npx args carry no version pin.

findings.summary: The command guard's denylist misses long-form and reordered flag variants of the catastrophic commands it blocks.
findings.description: `NewCommandGuard` matches `rm\s+-rf\s+/` and `git\s+push\s+--force`, so `rm --recursive --force /`, `rm -fr /`, `rm -r -f /`, and `git push -f origin main` pass unblocked even on the targets where the guard is wired. A regex denylist over a Turing-complete shell cannot be exhaustive, and the remit explicitly flags long-form flag coverage as a risk sensitivity.
policy_rule_text: Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.

evidence.snippet: Block(`rm\s+-rf\s+/`) and Block(`git\s+push\s+--force ...`) — no coverage for --recursive/--force long forms, reordered -fr/-r -f, or git push -f.

recommended_actions: Normalize the command (resolve flag aliases and argument ordering) before matching, or expand each pattern to cover the long-form and reordered variants of the blocked command.

findings.summary: The structured `Session.Findings` audit channel is defined and displayed but never written by any code path.
findings.description: `Session.Findings` is serialized and rendered by `yaah session show`, but `HandleHookEvent` only records tool calls, blocked calls, and modified files — it never appends a `Finding`, so security findings survive only as free-text in `BlockedCalls.Reason`. The remit requires security findings to be recorded to the durable per-session audit log, so the structured findings surface is effectively dead code.
policy_rule_text: Every hook event — tool calls, blocked commands, file modifications, and security findings — is recorded to the durable per-session audit log, whether or not it also raised an alert.

evidence.snippet: The session-update block records ToolCalls, BlockedCalls, and FilesModified (lines 129-156) but never appends to sess.Findings.
Findings []Finding is defined and shown by `session show` (main.go:521) yet has no producer anywhere in the code.

recommended_actions: Populate `sess.Findings` from the secret-scanner, linter, and comment-checker results inside `HandleHookEvent` so security findings are captured as structured records, not only free-text block reasons.

findings.summary: The Notion MCP provider embeds the operator's bearer token in cleartext into generated config environment variables.
findings.description: `notion.go` string-concatenates `Bearer <token>` into the `OPENAPI_MCP_HEADERS` env value, which is written verbatim into the generated agent config when Notion is enabled. The provider is opt-in and off by default, but when used it places a live credential into config files rather than referencing a vault or an env indirection.

evidence.snippet: OPENAPI_MCP_HEADERS is built by concatenating the bearer-token literal into the JSON header string, so the token lands in generated config env in cleartext.

recommended_actions: Reference the Notion token through an env-var indirection or a secret manager in the generated config rather than embedding the literal value.

positives.title: Enforcing command guard
positives.description: CommandGuard blocks catastrophic shell commands at PreToolUse and returns exit 2 to block execution; it is enabled by default and wired into the Claude, OpenCode, and Copilot outputs.

positives.title: Blocking secret scanner with remediation
positives.description: SecretScanner blocks Edit/Write/MultiEdit on thirteen credential patterns, and the default secret-remediation chain appends remediation guidance to the block.

positives.title: Durable structured session audit log
positives.description: session.Store writes an atomic per-session JSON record capturing tool calls, blocked calls, and files modified, detailed enough to reconstruct a session.

positives.title: Path-traversal-safe session IDs
positives.description: sanitizeID rejects path separators and relative components before any session file operation, preventing traversal via a crafted session ID.

positives.title: Pinned Go and remote-skill supply chain
positives.description: go.mod/go.sum pin exact dependency versions with integrity hashes, and every remote skill and agent is pinned to a full git SHA in defaults.go.

positives.title: TLS-only remote MCP
positives.description: The pulumi MCP server is configured as an https endpoint, satisfying the remit's TLS requirement for remote MCP connections.

positives.title: Clean MCP tool descriptions
positives.description: The seven built-in MCP tool descriptions state only what each tool does and carry no instruction-like language directed at the model (no tool-poisoning surface).

positives.title: Experimental fact-checker off by default
positives.description: Agent-type Stop/SubagentStop fact-check hooks install only when YAAH_EXPERIMENTAL_FACT_CHECK is set, keeping the higher-cost path opt-in.

rows.purpose: per-session tool calls, blocked calls, and files modified

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. yaah has a coherent, genuinely operative safety model — enforcing command and secret guards, a structured durable audit log, and disciplined dependency pinning — that places it above a hobby project in most categories. It is held to Partial by a single Zero-Trust uniformity failure (one of four generated targets ships without the protective hooks), a weaker command-guard denylist, an unpinned third-party MCP fetch path, and an absence of adversarial testing of its own protections.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: yaah is a narrow, non-inference config generator whose runtime tool surface is a seven-tool check-only MCP set plus five lifecycle hooks, matching the remit's Known Good Baseline; no general-purpose or domain-widening capability appears in `pkg/mcpserver/tools.go` or the generators.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: yaah performs no LLM inference and moves no external content into an LLM context, reading workspace files only locally to scan and lint; the main data-handling weakness is `pkg/mcp/providers/notion.go` embedding an operator token in cleartext into generated config env.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The command guard (PreToolUse block) and secret scanner (PostToolUse block with a remediation chain) are real enforcing controls — but only for Claude, OpenCode, and Copilot; `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` leaves Codex without them (PRAX-2026-07-17-001) and the guard's denylist is bypassable by flag variants (PRAX-2026-07-17-003).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Go dependencies are pinned in `go.mod` with a committed `go.sum` and every remote skill/agent in `pkg/harness/defaults.go` is pinned to a full 40-character git SHA; the gap is `context7`/`notion` MCP servers launched via `npx -y` with no version pin (PRAX-2026-07-17-002).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No evidence of adversarial testing of the protections; the unit tests present are functional correctness checks, and the command-guard denylist missing obvious `rm --recursive --force /` and `git push -f` variants indicates the guard was not adversarially exercised.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: `pkg/session/store.go` maintains a durable, structured, timestamped per-session JSON audit log capturing tool calls, blocked calls, and files modified, with atomic writes and path-traversal-sanitized IDs; the gaps are that `Session.Findings` is displayed but never populated (PRAX-2026-07-17-004) and Codex produces no tool-call trail.
